Daily HHS Accessibility Report — 2026-04-14#

Run ID: run-2026-04-14-a4b254fa03 · Status: partial · Source data: 2026-04-12 · Generated: 2026-04-14T09:49:27.301Z

View HTML/CSS/JS Code Quality report for this date →

About These Reports#

U.S. federal government website traffic data is publicly available at analytics.usa.gov, powered by the Digital Analytics Program (DAP) — a shared analytics service that tracks traffic across hundreds of participating federal agencies and publishes the data openly. This report focuses on the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and its sub-agencies, using HHS-specific traffic data from analytics.usa.gov/health-human-services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited HHS URLs as reported by analytics.usa.gov. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from analytics.usa.gov for HHS properties. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used HHS pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 8 days of data (2026-04-03 to 2026-04-13), government website accessibility scores have improved (+5.18 points, from 87.31 to 92.49). Performance scores have declined (-7.29 points, from 62 to 54.71).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.68 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 113 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

The following axe-core rules were most frequently violated across scanned URLs today. These patterns indicate systemic accessibility barriers present across multiple government websites.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

skip-link: Skip Link Functional Effectiveness

A skip link that points to a non-existent or non-focusable target provides no actual benefit and may confuse keyboard users who activate it and experience no apparent result. For screen reader and keyboard users on government websites with extensive navigation, a broken skip link means they must navigate through the entire repeated header every time, negating the accessibility feature entirely and creating a cruel illusion of accessibility that does not function.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard-only navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

tabindex: Focus Order Predictability

Positive tabindex values create unpredictable focus sequences that jump around the page in ways that differ from the visual reading order. For keyboard users and screen reader users on government websites, an erratic tab order makes it difficult to reliably navigate forms and interactive content, increasing errors and the time needed to complete tasks, and creating particular frustration for users with cognitive disabilities who need a predictable, logical interaction flow.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard-only navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard or switch access

label: Form Field Identification

Unlabeled form fields are the most common barrier preventing blind and low-vision citizens from completing government forms. When input fields lack programmatic labels, screen reader users cannot determine what information is being requested, making it impossible to apply for benefits, file taxes, register to vote, or access virtually any government digital service that requires form input. This single failure can block an entire service interaction.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on assistive technology
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control

button-name: Button Discoverability

Unlabeled buttons are silent black holes in the government digital experience. When a screen reader announces a button with no name, blind users cannot know what action it will perform. Submit, cancel, search, and navigation buttons without accessible names prevent citizens from completing applications, accessing benefits, or finding essential government resources independently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 13,426,796 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 820,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)
Icon Disability Group (Section 508 FPC) U.S. Prevalence U.S. Population Affected Page Loads with Barriers Est. Americans Excluded Today
Limited Reach and Strength 5.8% ~19,600,000 5,030,000 290,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 4,700,000 200,000
Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 6,150,000 130,000
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 4,720,000 110,000
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 6,050,000 60,561
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 6,050,000 18,168
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 2,894 136

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 102 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on April 14, 2026
Metric Estimated total (April 14, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 11 months, 14 daysExtra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Total: 30,145,661 seconds. 30,145,661 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~4.1 TB (173 copies of Wikipedia)Extra data is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. Across 102 URLs with weight data

LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic. Hover or focus the estimated totals for calculation details.

Content Density (Words per Megabyte)#

Words-per-Megabyte (WpM) measures how efficiently a page delivers readable content relative to its total download size. A low ratio indicates Digital Bloat—the page transfers far more data than the text it contains. Pages below 200 WpM are flagged for optimization review.

  • URLs with content metrics: 79
  • Average word count: 933
  • Average Words/MB: 777
  • URLs below 200 WpM threshold: 57
Content density for top URLs scanned on April 14, 2026 (up to 10 shown)
URL Word Count Words/MB

https://clinicaltrials.gov 22 15
https://acf.gov 239 77
https://nih.gov 250 143
https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov 77 36

Word counts are extracted from the page’s main article content using Mozilla Readability, which strips navigation, sidebars, and ads. Page weight is the total resource size measured by Lighthouse. Pages below 200 WpM may impose unnecessary data costs on users on metered connections.

Detected Technologies#

Technology signals detected from the network requests loaded by each scanned URL. CMS detection identifies WordPress, Drupal, and Joomla from characteristic asset paths. USWDS detection identifies use of the U.S. Web Design System.

Third-Party JavaScript Services#

Third-party scripts detected from network requests loaded by scanned pages. These services may affect page performance, user privacy, and security posture.

9 services marked ⚠ tracking send user data to third-party servers and may have privacy implications for site visitors.

Accessibility Statements (M-24-08)#

OMB Memorandum M-24-08 requires each federal agency to publish a digital accessibility statement that includes contact information for accessibility issues, known limitations, and a link to the agency Section 508 program page.

40 of 113 domains (35%) checked have a detectable accessibility statement at a standard URL path.

Required Federal Links (M-17-06)#

OMB Memorandum M-17-06 and the 21st Century IDEA Act require federal agency public websites to provide links to a Privacy Policy, Contact page, and FOIA page. These were among the compliance criteria benchmarked by the performance.gov federal website performance initiative (no longer active, but the underlying requirements remain in force).

36 of 113 domains (32%) have all three required link types detectable at standard URL paths.

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 201 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 820,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) HHS-84eeaaec

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~103KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~103,897 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 4,329,054 affected page loads) ~186KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~186,149 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 4,329,054 affected page loads)

<p class="date">

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-3e7fec37

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Estimated ~5,583 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 558,273 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1 HHS-2428d6f9

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link) HHS-b3698d5b

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-6eb16f7d

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

https://fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-ecc5de53

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-756f8154

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://clinicaltrials.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) HHS-413fb5f5

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Affected elements (7):

Element 1 HHS-c0b3ae90

Element path: li > ul > li > a.external

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov" class="external" target="_blank">

Label: HHS.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size because it is partially obscured (smallest space is 59.4px by 19.2px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 15.8px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-3c20b2e6

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Estimated ~1,824 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 75,982 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-67550629

Element path: div.usa-identifier__container > div.usa-identifier__identity > p.usa-identifier__identity-disclaimer > a.ext

Snippet:

<a href="http://www.hhs.gov/" class="ext" data-extlink="" target="_blank" rel="noopener nofollow noreferrer">

Label: Department of Health and Human Services

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.31:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #dfe1e2, surrounding text: #ffffff)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-16f544ab

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-f5894d5c

Affected elements (12):

Element 1 HHS-29d193dd

Element path: div.searchNav > ul.searchType > li.human > label

Snippet:

<label for="labeltype-human">

Label: HUMAN DRUGS

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.38 (foreground color: #a1dfff, background color: #2b789f, font size: 9.0pt (12px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element path: div.searchNav > ul.searchType > li.animal > label

Snippet:

<label for="labeltype-animal">

Label: ANIMAL DRUGS

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-1e1f4c3a

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~520People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~520 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 23,651 affected page loads) ~1KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~1,372 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 23,651 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-ad6cae95

Element path: div.footer-links > div.social > div.addthis_toolbox > a.email-share

Snippet:

<a class="email-share" href="mailto:?subject=DailyMed%20Website%20Information&amp;Body=I%20would%20like%20t…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (16px by 16px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 18px instead of at least 24px.
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) HHS-c41d9709

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

https://wwwnc.cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-a1e74b24

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~230People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~230 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 23,033 affected page loads) ~69People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~69 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 23,033 affected page loads) ~507People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~507 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 23,033 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-39eea2eb

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link) HHS-7c38a66b

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Element 1 HHS-8e6c4134

Element path: body.cdc-post-type--cdc-homepage > div#skipmenu > a.skippy

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-4cec813a

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://support.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 4

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-be8eb6e7

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~166People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~166 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 16,593 affected page loads) ~50People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~50 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 16,593 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-057a64fb

Element path: div.row > div.search-bar-container > div.search-bar > button#clearSearchButton

Snippet:

<button type="button" class="clear-btn" id="clearSearchButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-9aa9af0a

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Element 1 HHS-db816958

Element path: div.usa-width-one-third > article > a > h4

Snippet:

<h4>

Label: Cervical cancer survivor urges young people to get HPV vaccine

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-3ade9b96

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-5ddf9415

Element path: div.usa-grid > button#herodeck > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="/article/after-pancreatic-cancer-maria-menounos-makes-health-her-focus">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-2b55cb65

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

https://meshb.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) HHS-717c2546

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Element 1 HHS-892d4ecf

Element path: div.sm:flex > div.grow > div.flex > button.w-[20px]

Snippet:

<button class="w-[20px] h-[20px] " tabindex="0">

Label: Select to search

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (20px by 20px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://wonder.cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-2a5d99ee

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~31People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~31 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 3,112 affected page loads) ~9People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1 HHS-faacb845

Element path: div.col > div.wonder > div.wonder-notif > button.wonder-btn-close

Snippet:

<button class="wonder-btn-close" onclick="hideElements('.wonder-notif')">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-68222e9d

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Accessibility Findings

https://npin.cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 3

`[accesskey]` values are not unique (rule: accesskeys) HHS-cd019ff9

Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique. Learn more about access keys.

Element 1 HHS-efdc8857

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-87fc0aa6

Accessibility Findings

https://register.clinicaltrials.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 76

Axe findings: 3

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-f25f070c

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-fb4c12ea

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Affected elements (3):

Element 1 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="TEXT" name="Organization" value="" maxlength="60" size="30">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="TEXT" name="UserName" value="" maxlength="40" size="30">

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 3 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="PASSWORD" name="Password" value="" autocomplete="off" maxlength="128" size="30">

  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-a1061c45

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-05ea2ca5

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-a86abae4

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Estimated ~103 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 2,396 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-fff8fa8b

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input#P101_LOGIN

Snippet:

<input type="button" onclick="SubmitClick();" value="Login" class="btn-red disable-on-submit" style="width: 215px; min-width: 85%; margin-top: 28px; border-radius: 10px;" id="P101_LOGIN" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.04 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #da4f49, font size: 9.0pt (12px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-8021544f

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Disabilities affected: ~24People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~24 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,396 affected page loads) ~58People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~58 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,396 affected page loads) ~7People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~7 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,396 affected page loads) ~53People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~53 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 2,396 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-15ba1d86

Element path: td.disclaimer > div#P101_AGREE > div.apex-item-option > input#P101_AGREE_0

Snippet:

<input type="checkbox" id="P101_AGREE_0" name="P101_AGREE" data-display="" value="Y" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Form element has explicit <label> that is hidden
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2 HHS-13b027a6

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input#P_HP_NAME

Snippet:

<input type="text" id="P_HP_NAME" name="P_HP_NAME" class="text_field apex-item-text apex-hp" style="position: absolute !important; left: -99999px !important;" value="" size="30" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-2f59774b

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-062f9725

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-04e4a51c

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Element 1 HHS-afd21310

Element path: div.row > div#main > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.nlm.nih.gov/mesh/">

Label: MeSH

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.77:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #337ab7, surrounding text: #333333)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-c6b07fb6

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) HHS-47b8a646

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name) HHS-d52a16c2

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children) HHS-cc5ce3a9

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Disabilities affected: ~16People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~16 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,624 affected page loads) ~5People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,624 affected page loads) ~36People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~36 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,624 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-d117ae5a

Element path: div.row > div.col-md-6 > div > h3

Snippet:

<h3>

Label: Evidence Files Archival Collection

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-4f6a937a

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~39People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~39 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,624 affected page loads) ~70People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~70 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,624 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-ffcff372

Element path: div.col-md-6 > div > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://findingaids.nlm.nih.gov/repositories/ammp/resources/fdanj608">

Label: archival collection

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.43:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #326295, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://precision.fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-de034caf

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~15People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~15 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,500 affected page loads) ~5People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,500 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-2d1d3835

Element path: footer.grid > div.flex > div.ThemeToggle__StyledThemeToggle-bpcezo > button

Snippet:

<button data-active="true">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2 HHS-2d1d3835

Element path: footer.grid > div.flex > div.ThemeToggle__StyledThemeToggle-bpcezo > button

Snippet:

<button data-active="false">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-bb92d27d

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-f4ba814d

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-06d5ace9

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Affected elements (9):

Element 1 HHS-fd22fd04

Element path: div.meta-wrapper > ul.post-tags > li > a

Snippet:

  • Target has insufficient size (280.4px by 16.1px, should be at least 24px by 24px)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-ef80656d

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) HHS-d8012402

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~21People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~21 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 878 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-f7deea39

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1,maximum-scale=1,minimum-scale=1,user-sc…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-9bd59b3f

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~19People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~19 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 878 affected page loads) ~51People with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~51 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 878 affected page loads)

Snippet:

<a id="fb1" href="https://www.fda.gov/about-fda/about-website/internet-accessibility" target="_blank">

Label: Accessibility (Opens in new window)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (83.6px by 20px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 17.2px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://catalog.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-b7f30aa3

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~21People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~21 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 855 affected page loads) ~37People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~37 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 855 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1 HHS-b9e02af8

Element path: md-card.default-card > md-card-content > p > a.md-primoExplore-theme

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.nlm.nih.gov/tsd/catalog_help/catalogupdates/index.html" class="md-primoExplore-theme">

Label: Catalog Updates

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.79:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #326295, surrounding text: #3a3a3a)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://thecommunityguide.org

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-264122e8

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~7People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 673 affected page loads) ~2People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 673 affected page loads) ~15People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~15 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 673 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-772ffc28

Element path: div.row > div.col-md-12 > div.cdc-textblock > h3

Snippet:

<h3>

Label: Explore Popular Features of The Community Guide

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid

Accessibility Findings

https://meshb-prev.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) HHS-751c8b36

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~7People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 669 affected page loads) ~2People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 669 affected page loads) ~15People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~15 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 669 affected page loads)

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) HHS-7914bb92

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~7People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 656 affected page loads) ~2People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 656 affected page loads) ~14People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~14 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 656 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-f836b7b5

Element path: html

Snippet:

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 92

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes are not valid or misspelled (rule: aria-valid-attr) HHS-a2a6887a

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid names. Learn more about valid ARIA attributes.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-e5f557ed

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~14 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 641 affected page loads) ~37People with limited reach, strength, or stamina. https://jobs.cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

`<frame>` or `<iframe>` elements do not have a title (rule: frame-title) HHS-da85bb08

Screen reader users rely on frame titles to describe the contents of frames. Learn more about frame titles.

Disabilities affected: ~6People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~6 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 608 affected page loads) ~2People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 608 affected page loads) ~13People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~13 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 608 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-0c178a51

Element path: div.slick-track > div.card > div.embed-responsive > iframe.embed-responsive-item

Snippet:

<iframe class="embed-responsive-item" width="560" height="315" src="//www.youtube-nocookie.com/embed/Q90QD_wJ4zw?cc_load_policy=1&amp;wmode=opaque…" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen="">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has no title attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Accessibility Findings

https://nihms.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

`[accesskey]` values are not unique (rule: accesskeys) HHS-bc33f52e

Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique. Learn more about access keys.

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-9992e3d4

Element path: body > a#nws_header_accesskey_1

Snippet:

Label: NCBI Homepage

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document has multiple elements with the same accesskey

Element path: body > a#nws_header_accesskey_3

Label: Main Content

Accessibility Findings

https://circulatingnow.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-79dd38c5

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~5People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~5 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 517 affected page loads) ~2People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 517 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-02528cb0

Element path: div.header-nav-search > div.header-navigation > nav#site-navigation > button.menu-toggle

Snippet:

<button class="menu-toggle" aria-controls="primary-menu" aria-expanded="false">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

<img _ngcontent-ng-c2263394713="" src="/imgs/150/112/2883703/PMC2883703_6605702f1.png">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-47202c11

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-7425321e

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-ddc57050

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-3e2fc26f

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

https://mor.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 79

Axe findings: 4

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-88229b04

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~6People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 245 affected page loads) ~11People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~11 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 245 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-ec64c88b

Element path: div.prefix-overlay-inner > div#prefix-overlay-step1 > div.prefix-overlay-button-panel > button#prefix-dismissButton

Snippet:

<button class="prefix-overlay-action-dismiss" id="prefix-dismissButton">

Label: No Thanks

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.76 (foreground color: #3276b1, background color: #e5e3e3, font size: 9.0pt (12px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element 2 HHS-38b984a3

Element path: div.prefix-overlay-inner > div#prefix-overlay-step1 > div.prefix-overlay-button-panel > button#prefix-laterButton

Snippet:

<button class="prefix-overlay-action-later" id="prefix-laterButton">

Label: Remind Me Later

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.76 (foreground color: #3276b1, background color: #e5e3e3, font size: 9.0pt (12px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-74bfad51

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2People who are blind or have no functional vision.   • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Accessibility Findings

https://nccd.cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) HHS-843f6154

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~2People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 182 affected page loads) ~1People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 182 affected page loads) ~4People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 182 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-bac02171

Element path: div.cdc-mobile-menu-body > div.cdc-mobile-audience > div.accordion-item > div.accordion-header

Snippet:

<div class="accordion-header" aria-expanded="false" aria-controls="mobilemenu-related">

Label: Related Topics

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"

https://www.nihms.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

`[accesskey]` values are not unique (rule: accesskeys) HHS-13650a1a

Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique. Learn more about access keys.

Element 1 HHS-642f7115

https://docline.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-5f7f9feb

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~3People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 128 affected page loads) ~6People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~6 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 128 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-0391afcb

Element path: div.card-body > div > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.nlm.nih.gov/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">

Label: National Library of Medicine's

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.43:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0063a4, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element 2 HHS-0391afcb

Element path: div.card-body > div > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://nnlm.gov" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">

Label: Network of the National Library of Medicine (NNLM)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.43:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0063a4, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://nida.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 98

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-7415d252

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.
